segments.text: because in order to do back prop,
it normally you maintain the entire state
of the computation graph.
And so there are a common method to fix this
is basically you pass along the RNN state
from the previous sentence,
but you just don't do back prop into the previous sentence.
And this is called truncated back prop
or truncated back propitation through time.
And this allows you to essentially train models
with infinite context or at least models
that can pass along context infinitely,
even if you're not back propping into the data folder.
So of course, a problem with this over long contexts
is recurrent models can be slow
due to the kind of sequential dependence.
They're not ideal for running on GPUs or things like that.
And this is approved by recent architectures like Mamba and RWKV,
which are more conducive to GPU based training
while still maintaining linear time complexity.
And so I'm looking forward to talking about that
more in the future class.
So actually, if we take this idea of truncated
back prop occasion through time,
this can also be applied to transformers.
And there's a really nice paper, a transformer Excel,
also created by Zahung Day, who was formerly at CMU.
And what this does is it tends to fix vectors
from the previous sentence.
So if we have a standard transformer,
transformer Excel, normally what we do
in the standard transformer is each vector
attends back to all the other vectors
in the current context.
What transformer Excel does instead is,
when you have a new segment that you want to do
back prop into, you have a new segment
that you want to basically train over.
You also attend to all of the previous tokens
in the previous segment, but you don't get
back prop into them.
So this is essentially truncated back propiation through time
from the transformer perspective.
This is also really nice because what it allows you to do
is if you have a multi layer transformer,
it allows you to attend far back.
So if you look at the last layer,
it's attending two things in the previous context window.
But the second to last layer is attending to things
in the, not just one context window before,
but multiple context windows before.
And actually this allows you to very effectively
attend to very long contexts,
because each time kind of the context expands
in an exponential manner.
So recently there's a popular model called NISTRO
that I'm sure a lot of people have heard about.
And this is using sliding window attention,
which is essentially the same mechanism
proposed by transformer Excel.
So this method is still used in very practical systems.
Another paper that has been pretty influential
in this general area is something called Sparse Transformers.
And the way Sparse Transformers work
is instead of attending to every single previous state,
you attend to every N previous states.
And what this allows you to do is this allows you to essentially
create something like the strided confolutions
or pure middle recurrent neural networks
that I talked about earlier.
So what this looks like essentially is you have this,
like if you have a particular state,
it might attempt to all of the previous N tokens.
But then it also attends to all of the previous kind
of M chunks.
So you kind of have a combination of local and global attention.
And that local and global, but local and kind of longer range
attention.
And this can be very effective because you can attend to,
you know, much longer context with a minimal increase
in a computational complexity.
So another method that's a little bit like this
or it's very similar in spirit,
but slightly different in implementation
is something called the compressive transformer.
And in the compressive transformer,
you also have this idea of a local memory
and then a longer term compressed memory.
But you have an explicit compression step
that directly essentially generates
this compressed memory itself.
And so this is a little bit more flexible, I guess.
It allows you to take all the, you know,
relevant things from your local memory
and compress it down.
So it's another method that's worth thinking about.
Finally, there are some very interesting methods
that do low-rank approximations of transporters.
And so calculating the attention matrix is expensive,
but this is a matrix.
And because it's a matrix,
we can also approximate it with a lower-rank matrix.
And there's a couple of methods that do things like this.
The first one is something called a linear transformer
which adds low-rank linear projections
into the model in appropriate places.
And there's another one called nice-room transformer
which approximates using the nice-room method,
which is based on sampling landmark points.
So basically, the general idea behind this
is normally we do this kind of softmax
over, you know, a very large attention vector.
But instead, we can approximate the softmax
by having some low-rank vectors,
kind of like what we used in Laura
and nonetheless get a reasonable approximation
of the softmax used in attention.
Okay, so we're nearing the end of what I want to talk about today.
And finally, the thing that I would like to talk about
is benchmarks for long-pad next models.
And there's a few benchmarks.
One very well-known one is something called long-range arena.
This is a composite benchmark
containing mostly non-enhilvy tasks.
And it's definitely used for long sequence modeling,
but the results on long-range arena
actually tend to diverge a sub-watt
from the results that you get
for long-distance language modeling.
So in addition to this, another benchmark
that I personally like and have used a bit
is something called scrolls,
which combines together a whole bunch
of kind of QA-style or summarization-style tasks
that have very long contexts,
including over-nouratives or books
where government reports or other things like that.
So you can also take a look at this
if you're interested in kind of benchmarking longer-range models.
Okay, the final thing I'd like to talk about
is now that we have retriever models,
we have reader models, we maybe even have reader models
that can effectively use very long contexts,
like the ones that we retrieve over whole documents,
how do we effectively use them in our models?
So there was a very nice paper
by Nelson Liu at Stanford
that about a phenomenon that was coined lost in the middle.
And basically what it does is it demonstrates
that many, many different models,
including state-of-the-art models,
pay less attention to things in the middle
of long context windows.
And so if we have an answer and we put it in the first position
that in a concatenated context
or the 20th position in a concatenated context,
it tends to attend more to the ones at the beginning
or the end.
In contrast, the ones in the middle kind of get lost
and sitting lost in the middle.
And the problem with this is,
if we are doing something like retrieval in reading,
then it's maybe not such a huge problem
because we could just put the highest scoring documents
at the beginning, there might even be more affected
than concatenating lots of low scoring documents together.
But if we want to read a really long document
and synthesize something without doing
kind of another scoring step that can be an issue.
And also, our retriever is not perfect.
So we would like the model to read our model
to do a good job with the upwards-seted test.
So there are methods to ensure use of relevant context.
So of course, better retrievers make more relevant context.
You can do re-ranking or other things like that
and include the context that looks most relevant
or refine your reader model.
But there's also methods that can decide
whether context should be used in the first place.
So there are methods to decide whether to use,
whether to include passages or not.
And also recently we proposed a method to filter down
to parts and retrieve passages
to have only appropriate content.
And this is a model that we call Philco.
It basically filters the context down
to the most relevant content that we think is appropriate
and that allows us to get better results
when it's fed to the generator.
So that's all I have for today.
